title: Podman.exe needs massive amounts of memory
body:
### Issue Description

I'm facing Go out of memory issues while pulling an image from an internal GitLab package registry. Podman.exe eats up to 45 GB of RAM:

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/efec5b1f-f7ac-4e51-a9d4-d76e50f8c633)

After a while, my system runs out of swap and this happens:

```
Copying blob sha256:7f277ee8bed44805960a3eca67dd186c255d9be585f89363017eb20444df3779
Copying blob sha256:6b00f683ffee327087c50d3ca035f1022a7a68cf657105b8ef585626a041ca30
Copying blob sha256:c7ff1e1c28caf41f7d4d07c56a352f0d85193b99d74a99edeb82ae61674ba899
Copying blob sha256:1a53a7c98eebcdbb648fb905bb7a0e2b12b763b2798de6211ad0ba86fba00144
Copying blob sha256:9eb0376e37804613d236b4b066dacd275fcb5c7739d5ee932d8a48433841b417
Copying blob sha256:f764f77c995ea7858cc66dcf76b986382828a83e06455f41a17b6397fe9afdbb
runtime: VirtualAlloc of 17955807232 bytes failed with errno=1455
fatal error: out of memory
```

which makes sense.

See the memory usage here until I press ctrl+c:

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/f69e5c74-900f-4694-b915-ddc8989a3d75)


Key question: why and what eats up my memory? I've observed podman machine in parallel, nothing to worry:
```

   podman machine ssh free -h
               total        used        free      shared  buff/cache   available
Mem:           3.8Gi       896Mi       2.4Gi       3.3Mi       803Mi       2.9Gi
Swap:          1.0Gi          0B       1.0Gi
  podman machine ssh free -h
               total        used        free      shared  buff/cache   available
Mem:           3.8Gi       898Mi       2.4Gi       3.3Mi       828Mi       2.9Gi
Swap:          1.0Gi          0B       1.0Gi
  podman machine ssh free -h
               total        used        free      shared  buff/cache   available
Mem:           3.8Gi       898Mi       2.3Gi       3.3Mi       836Mi       2.9Gi
Swap:          1.0Gi          0B       1.0Gi
  podman machine ssh free -h
               total        used        free      shared  buff/cache   available
Mem:           3.8Gi       882Mi       2.8Gi       3.3Mi       358Mi       3.0Gi
Swap:          1.0Gi          0B       1.0Gi
  podman machine ssh free -h
               total        used        free      shared  buff/cache   available
Mem:           3.8Gi       885Mi       2.8Gi       3.3Mi       358Mi       3.0Gi
Swap:          1.0Gi          0B       1.0Gi
   podman machine ssh free -h
               total        used        free      shared  buff/cache   available
Mem:           3.8Gi       885Mi       2.8Gi       3.3Mi       358Mi       3.0Gi
Swap:          1.0Gi          0B       1.0Gi
  podman machine ssh free -h
               total        used        free      shared  buff/cache   available
Mem:           3.8Gi       889Mi       2.8Gi       3.3Mi       358Mi       3.0Gi
Swap:          1.0Gi          0B       1.0Gi
```

Note that the 4 GB total memory come from my trials to adjust the memory of the podman machine via .wslconfig. Increasing memory to 32 GB does not help, the system stills crashes

```
 podman machine ssh free -h
               total        used        free      shared  buff/cache   available
Mem:            31Gi       1.0Gi        30Gi       3.3Mi       292Mi        30Gi
Swap:          8.0Gi          0B       8.0Gi
Interesting thing:
```

Running podman machine shows the WSL-configured resources, i.e. 32 GB

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/c5067ee9-0894-4fc5-9b48-8dc49b4569b4)

But a shut down machine shows something different:

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/cded819e-6659-4b2c-bb26-b2dd99acb23b)

So what's correct, 2.15 GB or 32 GB?

I'm on Podman Desktop 1.18.0

Anything I can share to help debugging that problem?

PS, pulling the very same image via the Podman Desktop UI works!

Also, I created the same issue here: https://github.com/podman-desktop/podman-desktop/issues/12327#issuecomment-2828282313 but it was recommended to create the ticket here.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1.  pull image as described above

### Describe the results you received

Describe the results you received

### Describe the results you expected

Describe the results you expected

comments.body: this can be image dependent, so we will need to know what image you are pulling or a reproducer with a public image we can pull.
also do you observe this with just podman (you have PD turned off, not loaded, or uninstalled) ?

@baude 
Okay I will work on a reproducer image. 

I'm only using PD, tbh I didn't know that there's a way to run podman on Windows without PD...

Any idea why it works in the PD UI but not via CLI, which seems to be some kind of podman remote thing?

@baude 

I killed all podman-desktop instances in task manager and made sure that there's no icon left in the status bar and ran

` podman pull pytorch/pytorch:2.7.0-cuda11.8-cudnn9-runtime`

After a while podman.exe escalated again:

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/db691ed8-f0e9-421e-886e-1e99cea8f678)

I ran the same test with enabled and disabled proxy/vpn, no difference.

PS, running this in the `podman machine ssh` works:

```
#  podman pull pytorch/pytorch:2.7.0-cuda11.8-cudnn9-runtime
WARN[0000] Using cgroups-v1 which is deprecated in favor of cgroups-v2 with Podman v5 and will be removed in a future version. Set environment variable `PODMAN_IGNORE_CGROUPSV1_WARNING` to hide this warning.
Resolving "pytorch/pytorch" using unqualified-search registries (/etc/containers/registries.conf.d/999-podman-machine.conf)
Trying to pull docker.io/pytorch/pytorch:2.7.0-cuda11.8-cudnn9-runtime...
Getting image source signatures
Copying blob 051978b61f01 done   |
Copying blob 4313689287d3 skipped: already exists
Copying blob 4f4fb700ef54 skipped: already exists
Copying blob c7ff1e1c28ca skipped: already exists
Copying blob 4d8a346f85ca done   |
Copying config 99b0368edf done   |
Writing manifest to image destination
99b0368edf98a40315bdc4e7e37a25d3641d18338f7c9aa4824007c030a109f4
```

So it's "just" the podman.exe which seems to be the podman-remote binary? Is this the right repo to report issues about that here?

Thanks!

> So it's "just" the podman.exe which seems to be the podman-remote binary? Is this the right repo to report issues about that here?

Yes

Such a large memory leak in the remote client seems indeed very concerning, normally I would ask to SIGABRT the process so we see what is happening in the process quickly but that is not a thing on windows.

And it seems our internal --memory-profile isn't supported with the remote client either so that is also not an option, likely would not work anyway if the process does not finish. So I guess the best chance would be to run it through a debugger like delve https://github.com/go-delve/delve and check there where the memory is leaked.

@Luap99 I've installed delve and tried `dlv exec podman.exe`, but obviously no source code is available. Does using devle on the binary make sense or shall I use the go code (which adds more complexity to setup etc)?

Does this stack trace help?

Is this perhaps related to https://github.com/containers/podman/discussions/24736?
@jankap can I ask about the origin of the podman.exe binary being run?  Where did you get it?

@baude of course, I installed it via Podman Desktop via winget `winget install -e --id RedHat.Podman-Desktop` as described [here](https://podman-desktop.io/docs/installation/windows-install).

Interestingly, the checksums of the Podman Desktop delivered and manually downloaded podman.exe from https://github.com/containers/podman/releases/tag/v5.4.2 do not match:

Podman Desktop:

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/8a5c7197-5893-47e5-b877-122311d29bf2)
```
Name: podman.exe
Size: 49137480 bytes : 46 MiB
SHA256: e0f83768867d21ef82e747db0f28631e354132a871450d625701986107abc615
```

Downloaded from https://github.com/containers/podman/releases/tag/v5.4.2 

```

    at /home/runner/work/podman/podman/pkg/bindings/images/pull.go:93
```

That looks like the relevant part.

https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/ac12341046b570c24aaec708a7abe22bcae8f297/pkg/bindings/images/pull.go#L71-L100

The code looks not exactly that safe. What most likely happens is that you don't get EOF error so we never exit. And instead whatever error happens from Decode() is put into report.Error which then appends in later to the pullErrors array, thus the array grows indefinitely until you run out of memory.

So I guess it would be good if you could set a breakpoint there and find out what the error is Decode() is returning or compile from source and add a Println there or something.

```diff
diff --git a/pkg/bindings/images/pull.go b/pkg/bindings/images/pull.go

-       dec := json.NewDecoder(response.Body)

        var images []string
        var pullErrors []error
 LOOP:
        for {

                        if errors.Is(err, io.EOF) {

```

Mind trying with this again, it should dump the actual API responses to the output so we can see the response format.

> how is report filled at all? Looks like a local variable which is always default/empty? (but again, I have no clue about Go)

The Decode() call takes the address of the report var and the decoder parses the json message it got from the server and populates the report fields based on that.

I am not sure why you get `unexpected EOF` here, that likely suggest some kind of invalid json payload from the server that the diff above hopefully makes visible.
It could also be helpful if you could run `podman machine ssh journalctl -u podman.service -r` after it happens to fail to see if the server maybe logged some errors as well.

In any case I would think the error handling in the bindings is wrong, I guess the proper thing would be to exit right away on the first decode error. Looking at the underlying code it seems once the Decoder() hots an error all the following Decode() call return the same error so that explains we it just spins forever on the same error until we run out of memory.
(That is easy to fix but it doesn't explain what triggers `unexpected EOF` in your case)

Could it be related to https://github.com/moby/moby/issues/49513#issuecomment-2872261165 where Go 1.24 seems to have a problem?

Well that suggest the service is crashing, as per the service logs the podman remote service did not crash here?
`podman machine ssh journalctl -u podman.service -r` or `podman machine ssh journalctl --user -u podman.service -r` when the machine is rootless

And per info podman was compiled with `go1.23.7` so I that should rule go 1.24 out?

> Could it be related to https://github.com/moby/moby/issues/49513#issuecomment-2872261165 where Go 1.24 seems to have a problem?

Interesting, that but got reported to the Go upstream repo, and it seems to fix some podman crashes, too. See https://github.com/golang/go/issues/73141#issuecomment-2847963929

But it was Linux related.
